title: Hardware Failures at Scale
concept: In a cluster of 10,000 GPUs, individual cards fail daily; pretraining code must automatically save checkpoints and resume training without human intervention.

exercises.prompt: Why do we need to save checkpoints during AI training?

options.text: To make the AI run faster
To prevent losing weeks of work when a machine fails
To cool down the hot computer chips

exercises.explain: When training on thousands of computers, components will fail. Programs must save their work periodically so they don't have to start over from the beginning.

cards.title: Expect the Crash
cards.body: When you train an AI on thousands of GPUs, hardware failure isn't a rare accident—it's a daily certainty. If you don't plan for it, your training will crash constantly and you'll lose days of work!

cards.title: Automatic Checkpointing
cards.body: In large clusters of thousands of GPUs, hardware parts break constantly. To prevent losing weeks of work, AI training systems use automatic checkpointing—saving the AI's progress to safe storage so it can instantly resume if a machine crashes.

cards.title: The Caravan Analogy
cards.body: Think of training on a massive cluster like driving a giant caravan of 10,000 trucks across the country. If one truck gets a flat tire, you don't cancel the whole trip! You pull over, swap the tire (or load the goods onto a backup truck), and keep driving from the exact mile you stopped.

cards.title: Resuming in Action
cards.body: Imagine you are training a model on 10,000 GPUs. Suddenly, GPU #4,012 overheats and shuts down. Instead of crashing the whole system forever, the orchestrator detects the failure, replaces the broken GPU with a spare, loads the checkpoint from 15 minutes ago, and resumes training.

cards.title: The Flawless Hardware Myth
cards.body: Many people think supercomputers are perfectly stable and never break down. In reality, with 10,000 GPUs running hot 24/7, components fail every single day. Success relies on software that expects failure, not perfect hardware.

cards.title: Manual vs. Automated Recovery
cards.body: Compare manual recovery with automated checkpoint recovery during cluster failures.

left.text: Training crashes, notifications alert engineers, progress is lost, and someone must manually restart the cluster.

right.text: Orchestrator detects the dead GPU, replaces it, loads the latest checkpoint, and resumes training automatically.

exercises.prompt: Which of the following is true about training AI models on a cluster of 10,000 GPUs?

options.text: Hardware failures happen almost daily and must be handled automatically.
Hardware never fails if you buy expensive GPUs.
Training stops completely and engineers must restart it manually every time.
A single GPU failure will corrupt all previous progress permanently.

exercises.explain: With thousands of GPUs, the probability of at least one component failing daily is extremely high. Systems must be built to tolerate these frequent disruptions.

exercises.prompt: True or False: In a cluster of 10,000 GPUs, you should expect and plan for hardware failures to happen on a daily basis.
exercises.explain: Hardware failures are a statistical certainty when running thousands of high-power chips simultaneously.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing word to complete the strategy for handling hardware failures.
exercises.sentence: To survive daily GPU failures, training systems automatically save progress using periodic check_word.
exercises.explain: Regularly saving progress (checkpoints) ensures that when a GPU inevitably fails, the system only loses a few minutes of training time instead of starting over.

exercises.prompt: Identify the statement that shows a poor understanding of scaling and hardware fault tolerance:

options.text: Our training script saves state variables to a shared filesystem every 30 minutes, allowing auto-resumption.
If a GPU fails, we halt the entire project for the week and wait for a technician to replace it before saving.
The orchestrator automatically detects dead nodes and restarts the training job using the latest checkpoint.

exercises.explain: Option B sounds like an AI that does not understand cluster scale. In massive training runs, we do not stop the entire project for manual repairs; we rely on automated checkpointing and recovery.

exercises.prompt: You are managing a cluster of 10,000 GPUs training a new LLM. A GPU fails every 12 hours. What is the best strategy to keep training on track?

options.text: Stop the project and wait for engineers to manually replace the hardware each time.
Ignore the failures and let the AI train with corrupted mathematical data.
Save checkpoints to external storage and use scripts to auto-resume from the last save.
Buy higher-quality GPUs so that zero failures occur over months of training.

exercises.explain: At massive scale, hardware failures are guaranteed to happen frequently. Instead of manually restarting training every time a GPU fails, saving regular checkpoints to external storage and using automated restart scripts ensures training can continue seamlessly.
